title: Understanding the arbitral award
sub_topics: limits of arbitral award to maritime rights only

stances.claim: The 2016 Arbitral Award has three operative parts: (1) rejection of the nine-dash line and affirmation of freedom of navigation; (2) recognition of Philippine maritime entitlements (TS, CZ, EEZ, ECS); (3) holding that no disputed feature qualifies as an 'island' so China has no maritime rights even where it has built structures.
stances.rhetorical_move: doctrinal summarization into a triadic structure

stances.claim: Part 1 of the award is enforced de facto by great powers conducting freedom-of-navigation operations because it serves their interests; Parts 2 and 3 are left to the Philippines because no one else benefits from enforcing them.

Lord Palmerston's teaching governs international relations: there are no permanent friends or permanent enemies, only permanent interests — China's rhetoric is always calculated to its interests, and so is America's 'America First'.
stances.rhetorical_move: appeal to classical realist doctrine + historical illustration (WWII alliances)

stances.claim: The Arbitral Award by itself cannot oust China from occupied features because it covered only rights over water, not over land — and the Philippines lacks military capability to enforce its maritime rights independently.
stances.rhetorical_move: doctrinal limits of the award + power-asymmetry framing

cases: Republic of the Philippines v. People's Republic of China (2016 Arbitral Award)
laws_treaties: United Nations Convention on the Law of the Sea (UNCLOS)

signature_phrases: no permanent friends or permanent enemies, only permanent interests
rights over water, not over land
Ah, that's a good topic for another column
True indeed is the teaching of Lord Palmerston
decision_framework_signals: applies realist 'national interest' framework (Palmerston) to predict enforcement behavior of allies
applies doctrinal limits-of-jurisdiction analysis to distinguish what the award did vs did not decide
uses asymmetry-of-power lens to explain why the Philippines must depend on either great-power interest alignment or independent capability
